title: IICRC Water Damage Classes Explained — Classes 1, 2, 3 & 4 for Australian Restorers
description: Understand the four IICRC S500 water damage classes and how they determine equipment loads, drying strategies, and scope. Essential knowledge for Australian restoration technicians. RestoreAssist automates class-based equipment calculations for every inspection.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## What Are IICRC Water Damage Classes?

Under IICRC S500:2025, water damage is assigned a class — separate from its category — that describes how much water has been absorbed into the building materials and how difficult that water will be to remove. The class directly determines your equipment load, your drying strategy, and how long the job will take.

Damage class is often confused with damage category. Category describes contamination level (clean, grey, or black water). Class describes the volume of water absorption and the porosity of the affected materials. Both must be documented on every job.

## Class 1 — Least Significant

Class 1 involves minimal moisture absorption. The affected area is small, the materials involved have low porosity (concrete slab, vinyl flooring, part of a wall), and little or no wet carpet or cushion is present. Evaporation rates are low because there is limited exposed wet surface area.

A typical Class 1 scenario is a small leak at a toilet supply line that has affected only the vinyl floor and the base of a single wall cabinet. Equipment requirements are minimal — often one or two air movers and a small dehumidifier will suffice.

## Class 2 — Significant

Class 2 involves the entire room, including carpet and cushion. Water has wicked up walls to a height of less than 60 cm. The moisture absorption is significant, but all affected materials are low-to-medium porosity.

Class 2 is the most common presentation in residential water damage claims in Australia. A burst pipe that has soaked a bedroom — carpet, underlay, and wall bases — typically qualifies. Equipment requirements increase substantially: IICRC S500:2025 Appendix B tables guide you to approximately one air mover per 10–15 m² of affected floor area plus one dehumidifier per 40 m².

## Class 3 — Greatest Evaporation

Class 3 involves the greatest amount of water absorption and the highest evaporation potential. Water has wicked into walls above 60 cm, is present in ceilings, insulation, or structural framing, or the entire room has been saturated including upper wall materials. Ceiling-source losses (burst pipes above a ceiling) often present as Class 3.

Class 3 jobs require aggressive equipment placement, including directed air movers aimed at wall cavities and, frequently, injection drying systems. Wall cavity temperatures and relative humidity must be monitored separately from the room air.

## Class 4 — Specialty Drying

Class 4 is reserved for materials with very low permeance that require long drying times and specialised techniques. This includes hardwood timber floors (dense wood), plaster (not plasterboard), concrete structural members, and brick masonry walls.

Class 4 drying requires low-grain refrigerant (LGR) or desiccant dehumidification to reduce the ambient vapour pressure enough to draw moisture from the dense material. Standard refrigerant dehumidifiers are often insufficient. Extended drying periods of 10–21 days are not uncommon for Class 4 materials.

Under IICRC S500:2025 §9.3, the restorer must reassess the damage class during the job — a Class 2 loss that is not progressing may be reclassified as Class 4 if dense materials have absorbed significant moisture.

## Documentation Requirements

Each inspection report should record the initial damage class, the basis for that classification (affected area dimensions, wall wicking height, material types), any reclassification during the job, and the final drying confirmation data. This documentation chain is essential for insurance scope justification.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome back to RestoreAssist. Today we're covering one of the fundamentals — IICRC water damage classes. This is one of those things that trips up a lot of technicians, especially when they mix it up with water categories. They're two completely different things, so let's get clear on it.

Water category tells you about contamination — how dirty the water is. Water class tells you about absorption — how much water has soaked into the building materials and how hard it's going to be to get it out. You need both on every single report.

Class 1 is the least significant. Small area, low-porosity materials — think a small section of vinyl floor or the base of a wall unit. Not much wet surface area, so evaporation is limited. A couple of air movers and a small dehumidifier will usually get you there.

Class 2 is what you'll see most often in residential work. The whole room is affected — carpet, underlay, walls up to about 60 centimetres high. This is your typical burst pipe in a bedroom. Equipment requirements jump significantly here. IICRC S500 Appendix B guides you to roughly one air mover per 10 to 15 square metres plus one dehumidifier per 40 square metres.

Class 3 is where it gets more serious. Water has wicked above 60 centimetres, it's into the ceiling space, it's in the framing. A burst pipe above a plasterboard ceiling that's rained down into a room will often present as Class 3. You'll need directed air movers, possibly injection drying rigs, and you need to be monitoring wall cavity conditions separately from room air.

Class 4 is specialty drying territory. Dense materials — hardwood timber floors, solid plaster, brick masonry, thick concrete. These materials have very low permeance, meaning moisture moves through them very slowly. You need LGR or desiccant dehumidification to pull the ambient vapour pressure low enough to draw moisture out. Don't expect a quick turnaround — Class 4 drying can run 10 to 21 days.

One thing that catches people out: you can reclassify during a job. If you start on a Class 2 and you're not seeing the drying progression you expect, check whether dense materials are involved — you might actually be looking at a Class 4. IICRC S500 section 9.3 specifically requires you to reassess as the job develops.

In RestoreAssist, when you log your damage class on the inspection, the equipment calculator automatically uses IICRC Appendix B ratios to generate your equipment placement recommendations. Change the class, and the quantities update instantly.

If you want to see that in action, head to restoreassist.app and have a look at the equipment calculator — it'll save you time on every job.
